skill.name: assume-cloudformation-role
skill.description: Assume an AWS IAM role for CloudFormation work, extract temporary STS credentials, and export them for AWS CLI commands.
skill.summary: Prepare temporary AWS credentials for CloudFormation stack operations.
skill.author: Daisuke897

security_audit.summary: Static findings for external commands, environment access, and temporary file use are confirmed, but they align with the skill purpose. The main concerns are handling AWS credentials in a predictable temporary file and exporting privileged credentials into the shell environment. No prompt injection attempt or evidence of malicious exfiltration was found in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: Temporary AWS Credentials Written to Predictable Path
medium_findings.description: The command example writes STS credentials to /tmp/creds.json before parsing and deleting the file. This is legitimate for the workflow, but a predictable path can expose temporary AWS credentials to local users or stale-file mistakes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file path and credential parsing commands are explicit in the documented workflow. The risk is contextual because the file is deleted, but the temporary secret exposure is real.

medium_findings.title: AWS Credentials Exported Into Shell Environment
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, and AWS_SESSION_TOKEN. This is required for AWS CLI use, but environment variables can leak through shell history, subprocesses, logs, or later commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact AWS credential variable names are shown as outputs and export targets. This is a confirmed sensitive-data handling pattern with legitimate operational purpose.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded Privileged CloudFormation Role Defaults
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes a default IAM role ARN and profile for a CloudFormation write role. Users may run commands against an unintended account or a role with broad stack modification permissions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The role ARN, profile, and session name are explicit defaults. The impact depends on the user's AWS trust policy, so this is a medium-risk configuration concern.

low_findings.title: AWS Credentials Directory Reference Is a Prerequisite
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged ~/.aws/credentials and ~/.aws/config as sensitive file access. In context, the skill only lists them as places where the AWS CLI profile may already be configured, not as files to read or exfiltrate.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 72 is a prerequisite statement rather than a command. It still references sensitive credential locations, but no direct read or network transfer is described.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Finding Dismissed
low_findings.description: The scanner reported a weak cryptographic algorithm at line 3, but the line is metadata describing AWS IAM role assumption. No cryptographic algorithm or hash implementation appears there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced line is the skill description and contains no cryptographic code. This is a clear static-analysis false positive.

dangerous_patterns.title: External AWS CLI Commands Can Modify CloudFormation Stacks
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes AWS CLI examples for assume-role, create-stack, update-stack, and delete-stack. These commands are expected for the skill, but they can change or remove cloud infrastructure.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact AWS CLI operations are documented in command examples. Their risk comes from intended infrastructure write behavior, not from hidden behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Substitution Parses Sensitive Credential Values
dangerous_patterns.description: The shell command substitutions read credential values from a temporary file and assign them to environment variables. This pattern is functional but sensitive.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution syntax and credential field names are visible on the cited lines. The pattern is not malicious by itself, but it handles secrets.

content.value_statement: CloudFormation work often fails when temporary AWS role credentials are missing or expired. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through assuming an IAM role and preparing AWS CLI credentials.
actual_capabilities: Documents the required AWS CLI profile, role ARN, and session name inputs.
Shows how to call AWS STS assume-role for CloudFormation access.
Explains how to parse STS credentials with jq.
Shows how to export temporary AWS credentials for AWS CLI commands.
Provides example CloudFormation create, update, and delete commands.
Lists prerequisites for AWS CLI, jq, and source profile permissions.
limitations: It does not validate IAM trust policies or CloudFormation permissions.
It uses a hardcoded default role ARN and profile from the source documentation.
It writes credentials to a temporary file before deleting it.
It does not rotate, revoke, or securely store credentials after use.

use_cases.title: Prepare Deployment Credentials
use_cases.description: Assume a CloudFormation write role before creating or updating infrastructure stacks.

use_cases.title: Recover Expired AWS Sessions
use_cases.description: Refresh temporary AWS environment variables when an existing CloudFormation session expires.

use_cases.title: Standardize Stack Operations
use_cases.description: Give team members a repeatable role-assumption workflow before running AWS CLI stack commands.

prompt_templates.title: Assume the Default Role
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the assume-cloudformation-role skill to prepare temporary AWS credentials with the documented default profile and role.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup before a CloudFormation operation

prompt_templates.title: Use a Custom Role ARN
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the assume-cloudformation-role skill with my AWS profile and role ARN. Confirm the exact account and role before showing commands.

prompt_templates.title: Refresh an Expired Session
prompt_templates.prompt: My CloudFormation AWS session expired. Use this skill to guide me through refreshing the temporary environment variables.

prompt_templates.title: Review Before Stack Deletion
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill to prepare role credentials, then help me verify the active AWS identity before any CloudFormation delete-stack command.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review before destructive infrastructure changes

output_examples.input: Prepare my shell for CloudFormation updates.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the configured profile and role, explains the STS assume-role step, and reminds the user to verify the active AWS identity before updating stacks.
The assistant guides the user through re-running the role-assumption workflow and replacing the expired environment variables.

output_examples.input: I need to delete a CloudFormation stack.
output_examples.output: The assistant prepares role credentials, highlights the destructive nature of stack deletion, and asks the user to confirm account, region, and stack name.

best_practices: Verify the AWS account, region, and caller identity before modifying any stack.
Use a unique temporary file path with restrictive permissions when handling credentials.
Unset temporary AWS environment variables after the CloudFormation task is complete.
anti_patterns: Do not run the default role ARN without confirming it matches your AWS account.
Do not leave temporary credential files on shared systems.
Do not use this workflow for long-lived credentials or permanent access keys.

faq.question: Does this skill create AWS credentials?
faq.answer: No. It guides AWS STS role assumption, which returns temporary credentials when the source profile has permission.

faq.question: Can it modify CloudFormation stacks?
faq.answer: The skill includes commands that can create, update, or delete stacks when valid AWS credentials are active.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: It handles AWS secrets, writes a temporary credential file, and supports infrastructure write operations.

faq.question: Does it access ~/.aws/credentials directly?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The file is referenced as an AWS CLI profile prerequisite, not as a direct read command.

faq.question: What tools are required?
faq.answer: The documented workflow requires AWS CLI, jq, and a source AWS profile with sts:AssumeRole permission.

faq.question: Should I use the default role ARN?
faq.answer: Only use it if it is your intended AWS account and role. Otherwise, provide your own role ARN.
